description: v4.0.7-hardened expansion. Module 1 Board Texture Trainer at 251 scenarios across 14 family/suit combos with rebalanced suitTexture distribution and honest sourceConfidence per family/qtype. Module 2 (Flop C-bet IP) unchanged. Spot context: BTN open 2.5x vs BB call, 100BB SRP, flop only, NLH MTT chipEV. All scenarios audited per postflop_audit_rules.js.

question.prompt: On Aเนยเธ… Kเนยเธ 5เนยเธ (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. Open range contains far more A-x and K-x; BB 3-bets most strong A-x preflop.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open ~45% range includes A2s-AKs, A8o-AKo, K9s+, KJo+ เนโฌโ€ many cards that connect with A or K. BB calling range vs BTN open is roughly 30% (22-JJ, suited connectors, suited gappers, A2s-A9s, KTs/KJs, QJs, etc.). Critically, BB 3-bets AJo+, AQs, AKs, KQs, KJs at high frequency, removing them from the flatting range. Result: BTN's flop range is much more A-x-heavy and K-x-heavy.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN holds AA, KK, AK at full preflop frequency; BB 3-bets these heavily, so BB's flatting range contains far fewer combos. Top of range overwhelmingly favors BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players answer 'neutral' assuming pocket pairs and connectors balance things out. They don't เนโฌโ€ sets of 5 are rare in both ranges and BTN has many more pairs that connect with A and K specifically.

question.prompt: On Kเนยเธ… Tเนยเธ 2เนยย , who has nut advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. KK and AK dominate the top of range; BB 3-bets these often so they're sparse in BB's flatting range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open contains KK, KJo, KJs, KTs, KTo (some), KQ, AK at high frequency. BB calls vs BTN typically with 22-JJ and suited Broadways, but BB 3-bets KK, AK, KQs, KQo at high frequency.
explanation.nutLogic: Top of range hands on Kเนยเธ…Tเนยเธ2เนยย  are sets (KK, TT, 22), top two (KT), top pair top kicker (AK, KQ). BTN dominates KK and AK. TT is in both ranges but BB's KT combos are rare (KTo / KTs typically 3-bet or fold). 22 is in BB more often. Net nut advantage clearly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some assume 'TT and 22 are easier for BB to have' and answer 'caller' เนโฌโ€ but the dominant top-of-range hands (KK, AK) are heavily in BTN's range and outweigh the small-pair edge.

question.prompt: On Jเนยเธ… Tเนยย  9เนยเธ (highly connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB). Highly connected board favors BB's wider connector / suited-gapper range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range contains many JTs, T9s, 98s, 87s, J9s, T8s, 76s เนโฌโ€ all of which hit this board for top pair, two pair, sets, or straights. BTN opens these but at lower frequency in raw combos than the broader BB call range. BB also has 88, 77, 66, 55 etc. for sets and pair-plus-draw equity.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights (KQ, Q8, 87, 78) and two-pair combos (JT, T9, J9) appear at higher frequency in BB's range. BTN does have AA-QQ overpairs, but on this connected board overpairs are not nutted เนโฌโ€ they're often only marginal made hands.
explanation.commonMistake: BTN players often assume 'I raised, so I have range advantage' and over-c-bet here. That's a major leak เนโฌโ€ solver play has BTN c-betting only ~25เนโฌโ€35% of range here, often with polar sizings, because the board belongs to BB.

question.prompt: On 5เนยเธ… 4เนยเธ 3เนยเธ (low connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB). BB's range is dense in low pairs, low suited connectors, and made straights.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB calls vs BTN with 22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77, plus 76s, 65s, 54s, 43s (some), A2s-A5s, etc. Many of these make sets, two pair, top pair, or open-ended on this board. BTN's overpairs (TT-AA) are a thin slice of range and aren't even ahead of BB's many made hands.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets (33, 44, 55) are concentrated in BB. Made straights (76, 67, A2 for the wheel) are mostly in BB's range. BTN's nut advantage is essentially zero here เนโฌโ€ even AA is just an overpair on a heavily textured low board.
explanation.commonMistake: Big leak: BTN players who think 'low board = no one has it = always c-bet small' get crushed here. The whole BB calling range loves this board. Solver: BTN checks ~70%+ of range.

question.prompt: On Aเนยเธ… Aเนยเธ 6เนยย  (paired ace), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. With one of the two remaining aces, BTN's wider opening range contains far more trip-A combos.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open includes A2s-AKs and A8o-AKo. BB calling range has only A2s-A9s and very few off-suit aces (BB 3-bets AJo+, AQo+). With 2 aces on the board, only 2 unseen aces remain เนโฌโ€ and BTN has many more A-x combos to hold one of them.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-A is the nut equivalent here. BTN holds it at much higher frequency. Full house combos (66, AA เนโฌโ€ but AA is impossible with 2 aces on board) are essentially the same low frequency for both players, and 66 is mostly in BB's range. Net, BTN dominates.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players overweight 'BB has 66 = BB has nuts' and answer caller. Sets of 6 are 3 combos for BB and 3 for BTN เนโฌโ€ they cancel. The real edge is in trip-A frequency, which BTN dominates.

question.prompt: On 8เนยเธ… 7เนยเธ 6เนยย , how dynamic is this board?

explanation.short: Very dynamic. Every overcard makes a straight or massively shifts equity.
explanation.rangeLogic: Made straights already exist (T9, 95, 54). The turn or river: 5 เนยโ€ straight for 9-x and 4-x; 9 เนยโ€ straight for 5-x and T-x; T เนยโ€ straight for J-x and 9-x; J เนยโ€ straight for T-x; 4 เนยโ€ straight for 5-x; etc. Almost any non-paired card changes the equity landscape significantly.
explanation.commonMistake: Underestimating how many cards swing equity. The rule of thumb: if an overcard or undercard makes a straight for a meaningful slice of the calling range, the board is at least dynamic. Here, it's about as dynamic as flops get.

question.prompt: On 9เนยเธ 5เนยเธ 2เนยเธ… (low dry rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN เนโฌโ€ slight to moderate edge. Overpairs (TT-AA) dominate this static, disconnected low board.
explanation.rangeLogic: On 9-5-2 disconnected rainbow, the strongest hand class is overpairs. BTN has TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA in opening range. BB has TT-QQ for sure, KK and AA at much lower frequency (3-bets these often). BTN also has many overcards (A-x, K-x) that have backdoor + over-pair-when-paired equity.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets (99, 55, 22) are about equally distributed. Two-pair (95, 92, 52) is rare in both ranges. The nut hands here are sets and overpairs; BTN's overpair density gives the edge.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players answer 'caller' assuming 'BB has small pairs and 5x'. But BB has roughly the same set frequency, and BTN's overpair density wins on this static board.

question.prompt: On Kเนยเธ… Kเนยเธ 7เนยย  (paired king), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN เนโฌโ€ large edge. BTN has many more K-x combos for trips, plus overpairs.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open contains K9s+, KJo+, KQ at high frequency. BB's flatting range vs BTN open contains very few K-x combos because BB 3-bets KJ+ and KQs/KQo most of the time. With 2 kings on the board, the trips-K combos remaining go heavily to BTN.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-K is the dominant top-of-range hand. BTN dominates trips-K. KK is impossible. 77 is in BB much more often (BTN often opens 77 but BB has it as a flat combo more frequently). Boats from KK would matter but KK is on the board. Net: BTN has clear nut advantage too.
explanation.commonMistake: Underweighting the impact of BB 3-betting K-x preflop. The 'BB has random K' myth fails on board where BB filtered most K-x out preflop.

question.prompt: On Aเนยเธ… Kเนยเธ 5เนยเธ (two-tone), what is the optimal c-bet strategy for BTN?

choices.label: Mixed เนโฌโ€ small bet ~50%, check ~50%

explanation.short: Small (33%) high frequency. Two-tone adds some draw equity but BTN still dominates with range adv + nut adv.
explanation.rangeLogic: Same dynamics as A-K-5 rainbow, with the addition that some BB combos pick up flush draws (e.g., QdJd, QdTd). This slightly equalizes equity but doesn't flip range advantage. BTN still bets ~75% of range.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN has nut flush draw combos (AdXd in opening range เนโฌโ€ though many AdKd-type combos are now blockers to BB's flushes). Nut advantage stays with BTN.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is best because (1) BTN has range advantage so betting most of range is +EV, (2) the small size keeps the pot manageable on a board where flush draws can complicate big-pot decisions, (3) it extracts thin value from BB's many marginal calls.
explanation.commonMistake: Going polar big on this board to 'charge the flush draw' loses EV เนโฌโ€ small high-freq value bets accomplish denial AND value extraction simultaneously, while big bets fold out the marginal calls that small bets get.

question.prompt: On Qเนยเธ… 9เนยเธ 6เนยย  (semi-connected, rainbow), what's the optimal c-bet strategy?

choices.label: Mixed เนโฌโ€ small ~50%, check ~50%

explanation.short: Mixed small / check (~50/50). Modest range advantage with shared nut advantage = balanced strategy.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN has overpairs (TT-AA) and Q-x (KQ, AQ) for top pair. BB has 99, 66, sets, plus T8s, 87s, 76s, 65s for two-pair / draw equity. Range advantage tips slightly to BTN but not as dominantly as on A-high or K-high dry.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets are roughly even. Top pair (Q-x) leans BTN. Straight combos (87, T8) lean BB. Net nut advantage is roughly neutral.
explanation.sizingLogic: Mixed strategy: small bet with stronger Q-x and overpairs for value, check with weaker overpairs (88-TT) and air. Polar big is wrong because BTN's nut advantage isn't strong enough to support a bluff-heavy big sizing.
explanation.commonMistake: Defaulting to 'small high freq' on every board where BTN has range adv. On semi-wet boards like this, mixing in checks protects the checking range and avoids over-betting medium hands into the caller's many sets and draws.

question.prompt: On Tเนยเธ… 8เนยเธ… 3เนยเธ… (monotone), who has nut advantage?

explanation.short: BTN เนโฌโ€ slight nut edge from holding more Aเนยเธ…-x and Kเนยเธ…-x suited Broadways.
explanation.rangeLogic: Range advantage on monotone boards is roughly neutral here เนโฌโ€ BB's range has many low/mid hearts (87s, 76s, 9เนยเธ…7เนยเธ…) but BTN's wider range still picks up many flush combos.
explanation.nutLogic: The nut flush is Aเนยเธ…-X เนโฌโ€ BTN's range (A2s-AKs all suited) contains all suited Aces, and many of those are with hearts. BB's flatting range has fewer Aเนยเธ…-X combos because BB 3-bets ATs+, AJs+ at high frequency. BTN holds the nut flush (Aเนยเธ…Xเนยเธ…) more often. Second-nut flush (Kเนยเธ…Xเนยเธ…) similarly favors BTN. Net nut advantage = BTN, but moderate not crushing.
explanation.commonMistake: 'Monotone always means caller advantage' is wrong. On low monotone (5h4h2h), caller has more เนโฌโ€ but on T-high or higher monotone, the player with more high-suited hands (usually the raiser) holds nut advantage.

question.prompt: On 8เนยย  7เนยย  5เนยย  (monotone, low connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB) crushes this board. BB has more low pairs, more 5เนยย /7เนยย /8เนยย  holdings, and more flush combos.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range contains many small suited connectors and one-gappers (87s, 76s, 65s, 54s, 86s, 75s) เนโฌโ€ many of which include the spade for a flush. BB also has 22-77 for sets. BTN's range is wider but heavily favors broadway and high cards that don't connect with this board.
explanation.nutLogic: Made flushes เนโฌโ€ BB's range contains low-spade flush combos (e.g., 9เนยย 6เนยย , Jเนยย 4เนยย  if in flatting range). Straight combos (96, 64, 69s etc.) more frequent in BB. Nut flush Aเนยย X is roughly even since BB's Aเนยย Xเนยย  frequency is meaningful (A2s-A9s in BB, Aเนยย -x). On low monotone, the calling range really does eclipse the raiser.
explanation.commonMistake: BTN players who 'always c-bet small' on monotone get punished here. Solver: BTN c-bets ~15เนโฌโ€25% of range max, often as a small block bet with overpairs.

question.prompt: On Jเนยเธ… Tเนยเธ… 4เนยเธ (two-tone, semi-connected), how dynamic is this board?

explanation.short: Dynamic (3). Many straight draws (KQ, Q9, 98, 87) and an active flush draw.
explanation.commonMistake: Underrating two-tone semi-connected boards. The flush draw alone makes any board at least dynamic. Add the open-enders and gutshots to KQ, Q9, 98, 87, Q8 and you have constant equity churn.

question.prompt: On Qเนยเธ 9เนยเธ 4เนยเธ… (semi-dry rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN เนโฌโ€ moderate edge. Q-x (KQ, AQ, QJ) and overpairs (KK, AA) carry the range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN opens KQ, AQ, QJs at high frequency. BB's calling range has QJs (some), JT, T9, QTs sparingly, but BB 3-bets KQ, AQ heavily. BTN's range is denser in top-pair Q-x and overpairs. BB has more 99 sets and small connector equity (98s for two pair, 8-x for second pair).
explanation.nutLogic: Sets QQ vs 99 vs 44 เนโฌโ€ QQ favors BTN (BTN opens at full frequency, BB sometimes 3-bets QQ). 99 even. 44 slight BB lean. Top pair Q-x clearly BTN. Net nut advantage modestly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some assume neutrality because of '9 in BB's range' เนโฌโ€ but BTN's overpair density and Q-x density outweighs the second-pair argument.

question.prompt: On Aเนยเธ… Jเนยเธ… 3เนยย  (two-tone), what sizing family is best for BTN?

explanation.short: Small high-freq (range_small). Two-tone adds some draw equity but A-high range advantage stays huge.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN dominates A-x range hugely. Two-tone slightly equalizes via flush draws (BB picks up Qเนยเธ…Tเนยเธ…, Kเนยเธ…Tเนยเธ… etc.) but range advantage stays clearly BTN.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN holds Aเนยเธ…-X for nut flush draws. Made flush is impossible on flop. Top of range still dominates BTN.
explanation.sizingLogic: Range_small (33%) is best because BTN can bet the entire range profitably. Polar big (75%) is acceptable as a secondary line for the very polar combos (sets + nut bluffs) but isn't the main strategy. Check-heavy bleeds EV.
explanation.commonMistake: Going too polar 'because there's a flush draw' loses EV เนโฌโ€ small bets with nearly the entire range still works on A-high two-tone.

question.prompt: On 9เนยเธ… 8เนยเธ 7เนยเธ (highly connected), what's the best c-bet strategy for BTN?

explanation.short: Check heavy. BB has range advantage and many made hands; betting most of range bleeds EV.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range has many T9s, 98s, 87s, 76s, J9s, T8s, plus 99, 88, 77, 66 etc. Many made straights, two-pair, sets. BTN's overpairs (TT-AA) are now medium hands at best.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights live. Sets concentrated in BB. BTN's nut hands (sets of 9, 8, 7, made straight with JT) exist but at lower combo count than BB's nut hands.
explanation.sizingLogic: Check ~70%+ of range with everything except the strongest hands and selected polar bluffs (e.g., AQ with backdoor straight + overcards). When betting, use big sizing (~75%) polarized.
explanation.commonMistake: Range-c-betting small here is a critical leak เนโฌโ€ BTN gets check-raised constantly by BB's many made hands and draws, and the small bet doesn't fold out enough equity.

question.prompt: On Kเนยเธ… Jเนยเธ 8เนยย  (semi-connected, rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN เนโฌโ€ moderate edge from K-x and J-x density.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN opens KJ, KQ, KT, AK, KJs+ at high frequency. BB calls some KT, KJ but 3-bets KQ/KJ often. BTN dominates K-x. J-x is closer (BB has JT, J9 etc.) but BTN still has more KJ, AJ, QJ.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets KK, JJ, 88 เนโฌโ€ KK heavily BTN, JJ split (some 3-bet), 88 slightly BB. Two pair KJ heavily BTN. Top pair K leaning BTN. Top of range BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Underrating K-high broadway boards. Even when there's some connectedness, the broadway-heavy nature still favors BTN.

question.prompt: On Tเนยเธ 9เนยเธ 2เนยย  (rainbow, T9 connected), how dynamic is the board?

explanation.short: Semi-static (2). T9 is connected but no flush draw and 2 is disconnected เนโฌโ€ limited equity swings.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating any two-card connection as 'dynamic' overestimates equity churn. T-9 is connected but the 2 doesn't extend the structure, and rainbow eliminates flush dynamics.

question.prompt: On 6เนยเธ 5เนยเธ 4เนยย , what sizing family is best for BTN?

explanation.short: Check heavy. BB owns this board on every dimension.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB has all the connectors (76, 65, 54, 43, 87, 86), all the small pairs (44, 55, 66, 33, 22), and many flush-draw combos. BTN's overpairs are weak; BTN's broadway hands are mostly air.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights and sets concentrated in BB. The 'top' of BTN's range here (AA, KK) often has under 50% equity vs BB's full continuing range.
explanation.sizingLogic: Check ~65เนโฌโ€75% of range. When betting, polarize big with sets/straights and selected high-equity bluffs (e.g., AcQc combo draw). Small bets here get raised mercilessly.
explanation.commonMistake: Critical leak: range-c-betting small. The BB will check-raise relentlessly and BTN can't continue with most of the range. Polar big with low frequency is the only profitable bet line.

question.prompt: On 7เนยเธ 7เนยย  3เนยเธ (paired low), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Split / mostly neutral. BTN has overpairs, BB has more 7-x and small pairs เนโฌโ€ they roughly balance.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN's overpair density (88-AA) is real but BB's 7-x combos (97s, 87s, 76s, 75s, 74s, 73s rare) and 33 sets (BTN's 33 mostly opens too เนโฌโ€ even split) keep things close. BB has slightly more underpairs (22, 33, 44, 55, 66) which all have meaningful showdown value but trail 88+.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-7 require holding a 7 (77 is impossible เนโฌโ€ both 7s are on the board). Both ranges contain 7-x suited combos (87s, 76s, 97s, A7s, K7s) at similar rates; BB's wider flatting range gives a small edge on low 7-x density. Full house (33 + 7-x kicker) is rare in both ranges since pocket 33 is sparse and 3-x without a 7 doesn't make a boat. Overpairs (88-AA) lean BTN at the very top because BB 3-bets QQ+ heavily เนโฌโ€ BB's overpair density caps near JJ. Net nut advantage is roughly neutral with a slight BTN edge from premium overpairs.
explanation.commonMistake: Overweighting overpairs as 'range advantage' on paired low boards. Underpairs and trips combos balance things significantly. The texture supports a c-bet because BTN can fold equity from BB's many missed broadways, but the underlying range advantage is split.

question.prompt: BTN with Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ on Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ5เนยเธ. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. Top two with the entire range bet เนโฌโ€ small high-freq value plus protection.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN bets the entire range small here. AK is value plus blocker เนโฌโ€ by holding both A and K, hero blocks BB's nutted A-x and K-x continues, so the bet folds out air (which is what BB has most often) and gets called by worse A-x and K-x.
explanation.handLogic: Top two is too strong to check เนโฌโ€ protection from runner-runner draws and value from K-x marginals is essential. The hand benefits from getting 3 streets of value when a low or rag turns.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is best because BTN's whole range bets small. Big (75%) is acceptable as part of the polar mix but small is preferred เนโฌโ€ small extracts value from K-x and pocket pairs that fold to big.
explanation.commonMistake: Slowplaying top two on a dry board because 'I'm so strong.' This loses value เนโฌโ€ there's almost nothing for opponent to outdraw, but plenty of K-x and pocket pairs that pay off.
explanation.takeaway: Top two pair on dry A-high -> bet small high frequency for value.

question.prompt: BTN with Qเนยย Qเนยเธ on Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ5เนยเธ. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. Mostly bet for thin value + denial; checking is acceptable as part of the mix.
explanation.rangeLogic: On Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ5เนยเธ the GTO bet-frequency is high (~75%+). QQ wants to bet because it has equity vs many BB calls (bluff-catching with K-x high might call) and gains from denying equity to BB's overcards (J-x, T-x with backdoors).
explanation.handLogic: QQ here is not an overpair เนโฌโ€ it's an underpair to top two of board. But it's still ahead of BB's many K-x bluff-catchers (BB rarely has K-x given preflop; more often pocket pairs 22-JJ that QQ beats and overcards that miss). Check has merit if you want to keep weaker hands in for value, but most of the time bet small.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) keeps the pot manageable when BB has K-x or A-x. Bet 75% is wrong because it folds out the J-x, T-x, low pairs that QQ beats and only gets called by what beats it.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking QQ 'because I'm scared of A-x' is a common leak. BB's range is mostly missed broadways and small pairs เนโฌโ€ QQ bets small for value and protection.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair on dry A-high -> small bet for thin value; worse pairs and ace-light call.

question.prompt: BTN with 7เนยเธ…6เนยเธ… on Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ5เนยเธ. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. In a small high-freq strategy, bluff with backdoor equity is part of the bet range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN bets ~75% of range small here. 76s has backdoor straight (with 4 or 8 to come) and backdoor flush (with hearts) เนโฌโ€ enough equity-when-called to bet for fold equity now.
explanation.handLogic: 76s is air on the flop but has multiple backdoor draws, making it a clear small-bluff candidate within the small high-freq strategy. Air without backdoors checks; air with backdoors bets.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is the strategy. Big (75%) is wrong เนโฌโ€ bigger bluffs lose more when called and don't extract more fold equity than 33% on this dry board.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking pure air 'because I have nothing.' If BTN bets the value range small, BTN must also bluff small with the backdoor combos to balance เนโฌโ€ otherwise the value range is too transparent.
explanation.takeaway: Air with bdfd + bdsd on dry A-high -> small range stab as part of high-frequency strategy.

question.prompt: BTN with Tเนยเธ9เนยเธ on Aเนยเธ…Kเนยเธ5เนยเธ. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. T9s with backdoor flush + backdoor straight + two overcards is a clear small-bet bluff.
explanation.handLogic: T9 of diamonds: backdoor flush (need 2 diamonds), backdoor straight (need J + Q or J + 8 to come), and Q/J overcards that can pair for showdown value. This is one of the best bluff candidates in BTN's range เนโฌโ€ multiple backdoor equity sources.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) gets folds from BB's air (J-x, Q-x, T-x air without backdoors) and keeps pot small for backdoor realization. Big (75%) folds out the same hands but commits more chips to a bluff เนโฌโ€ net negative.

explanation.rangeLogic: A-high or K-high two-tone disconnected board. BTN open carries far more A-x and K-x combos than BB's preflop call range, and BB 3-bets dominant top-pair combos preflop. Range advantage clearly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating two-tone exactly like rainbow (range-small always) misses value vs. BB's flush draws; check-heavy concedes the board to BB unnecessarily.

question.prompt: With AhKh on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair top kicker on a dry A-high board. Bet small with high frequency for value.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN has clear range and nut advantage on A-high dry rainbow boards. Small high-frequency c-bets dominate.
explanation.handLogic: AK is the top of BTN's range here. It wants to bet for value against worse Ax, K-high floats, and pocket pairs that defend. Slow-playing risks giving up streets where BB folds out worse hands cheaply.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts value from a wide thin-calling range without folding out the underpair / K-high portion. Big sizing chases away the marginal hands AK actually beats.
explanation.commonMistake: Overbetting with AK on dry A-high boards. The pot doesn't need to grow this fast and worse hands fold to big bets. Bet small with high frequency.
explanation.takeaway: Strong top pair on dry A-high โ’ bet small with frequency.

question.prompt: With 7c6c on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with backdoors on dry A-high. Range-stab small as part of high-frequency c-bet.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range-bets dry A-high boards at high frequency. Air hands with backdoor equity carry the bluff frequency.
explanation.handLogic: 76 has zero showdown value but enough backdoor equity (BDFD + BDSD with the 8 on board) to continue as a bluff if called. Checking gives up the stab equity for nothing.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the cost of the bluff low while still folding out a meaningful portion of BB's air and weak underpairs. Big sizing burns chips on a hand with no realisation behind it.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting air on a dry A-high board. The board class wants small high-frequency, not polar-big.
explanation.takeaway: Dry A-high with air + backdoors โ’ bet small as part of the range-stab.

question.prompt: With 9d9s on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.handLogic: 99 beats only worse pocket pairs and missed broadways. Betting makes weaker hands fold (we get nothing) and stronger hands (Ax) call (we lose). Checking gets us to showdown cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting because we have a pair. Medium pairs on A-high should check back the majority of the time โ€” pot control is the whole point.
explanation.takeaway: Mid pocket pair on A-high โ’ check for pot control.

explanation.handLogic: KhQd has 9 outs to a K-high or higher flush, plus 3 outs to a Q-pair (some of which improve us to top pair). The Kh blocker reduces a small slice of BB's continuing range. But the hand is dominated when the flush completes โ€” Ah-X always beats us โ€” so betting big is wrong, and check is fully GTO.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) at moderate frequency is fine for the bet-portion of the mix, taking thin equity-denial value. Big is wrong because it inflates the pot when we get called by hands that mostly beat us (made flushes).
explanation.commonMistake: Treating Kh on monotone hearts as a 'made K-flush.' Hero has 4 hearts (1 hero + 3 board) โ€” that's a flush DRAW, not a made flush. Need a 5th heart on turn or river.
explanation.takeaway: K-flush DRAW on monotone โ’ mixed: small bet or check; bet_big is rarely correct.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds zero hearts โ€” no flush blocker

question.prompt: With 9d9c on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Underpair with zero hearts on monotone. Check, accept showdown โ€” no flush, no blocker, no bet.
explanation.rangeContext: Without a heart, our equity to make the flush is 0. We're a pure bluff-catcher against any heart and a small favourite over air.
explanation.handLogic: 99 beats 22-77 with no heart and pure air. Betting only gets called by hands that beat us (any heart, J-x, sets). Check, fold to most pressure.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with underpairs on monotone 'because we have a pair'. Without a flush blocker, the pair is too vulnerable.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair with no heart on monotone โ’ check, don't bluff yourself.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero has 1 heart (6h) + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts โ’ flush DRAW (not made). When the flush completes, the 5-card flush is at-best Jh-high (the highest heart in the 5-card combo), and any opponent heart higher than 6 dominates this draw. Plus a gutshot to 7 for 6-7-8-9 straight (or straight flush if 7h hits).

question.prompt: With 6h5c on Jh 8h 4h, BTN's recommended action is to check despite holding a flush draw plus a gutshot. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Give up โ€” flush draw is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range
Pot control โ€” keep pot small with marginal equity
Value โ€” bet to get worse to call
Equity realization โ€” bet to leverage fold equity

explanation.short: Low flush DRAW is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range โ€” check to give up cheaply.
explanation.rangeContext: On monotone hearts, hero with only the 6h holds the lowest possible flush DRAW (not a made flush โ€” hero has 4 hearts, needs 5). When the flush completes on turn or river, every higher heart in BB's range (7h-Ah) beats us.
explanation.handLogic: 6h5c has 9 outs to complete the flush, but most of those outs make a hand that LOSES to BB's higher heart draws or holdings. Plus the gutshot (7) adds 4 outs (3 if we don't double-count 7h). Net effective equity is much lower than a normal FD because of the reverse-domination problem. Betting bluffs into a stronger range; checking gives up cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating the 6h on monotone as a 'made low flush' (it isn't โ€” it's a low FD), or betting the FD 'for fold equity' when most of the outs we hit are dominated. Worst of all: betting for 'value' โ€” we have neither a made hand nor positive equity vs calling ranges.
explanation.takeaway: Reverse-dominated low flush draw on monotone โ’ check / give up; betting for value is the trap to avoid.
